trajectory_summary: The call moves from neutral to a conditional booking, with Dave warming organically through his prior solar experience and then cooling slightly at scheduling. Bart completes the core verify beats efficiently and lands a tentative Wednesday appointment, but the booking is fragile: Dave verbally flagged it as tentative, the button_up protocol was not completed, the bill is unresolved, and a live roofing cross-sell signal was acknowledged but not captured. The call ends with Dave more curious than when it started — the trajectory is mildly positive — but the sit is at real no-show risk.
outcome_dispute: The primary outcome tag is 'unknown' per metadata. Based on the transcript, the correct tag is 'tentative_appointment' — Wednesday 11AM was verbally agreed but explicitly qualified by Dave as 'tentatively.' This is not a firm booking. The bill is also unresolved, which on a paid lead would mean the appointment cannot be confirmed per codex lead_types_and_bill_collection_rules. Lead type is unknown here, but the fragility is real regardless.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Button-up protocol completion on a reluctant scheduler
primary_coaching_focus.why: Dave flagged the booking as tentative and had already said three times he couldn't commit to a day. Bart got the time on the calendar but left every button_up anchor undeployed — no verbal handshake, no computer/no-driving requirement, no one-hour block statement. On a customer who plays every day by ear, these are the only structural tools that convert 'tentatively' into a sit. This is the highest-leverage coaching gap on this call because it directly affects whether Bart gets paid.

key_moments.quote: Hey, Dave. This is Bart Drum with Ion. I was just calling because we had a request here you're looking at solar for the home. How you doing?
key_moments.stewart_read: Bart opens with a generic 'we had a request' frame and skips any legitimacy establishment. No Tesla framing, no state mention, no 'certified installer' anchor. The codex intro template exists precisely to answer 'is this a scam?' in the first 30 seconds — Bart's opener does none of that work. The call proceeds without resistance here, but that's Dave's disposition, not Bart's technique.

Dave says 'ComEdair' — not a recognized utility name. ComEd (Commonwealth Edison) serves northern Illinois; 'ComEdair' is likely a mispronunciation or mishearing. Bart accepts it without clarification ('Okay. Sweet.'). If the utility name is wrong, bill collection instructions, net metering context, and any utility-specific framing the closer uses will be built on a bad foundation.

key_moments.quote: I think it's, like, 400 a month.
key_moments.stewart_read: Bart captures the $400 figure and immediately moves to a soft savings frame ('cut that back at least, save you some money'). He does not flip the bill into emotional alignment-against-the-utility. The codex pattern is capture + acknowledge + flip; Bart delivers capture + soft-acknowledge + move on. Dave has already told Bart he had a $16 summer bill on his other house — the flip was available and would have landed hard: 'You already know what solar does to a bill. Imagine what it does to $400 a month.' That line was available at 02:14 and was not used.

key_moments.quote: $16 summertime bill sounds really good with me. Yeah.
key_moments.stewart_read: Dave volunteers a concrete, emotionally positive outcome from 26 years of prior solar ownership. This is the highest-quality enthusiasm signal in the call — a customer who already believes in the product, has lived the savings, and is motivated to replicate it. Bart responds with 'Awesome. I love it.' and moves on. He does not anchor on it, does not use it to deepen the interest question, and does not reference it again when the $400 bill comes up 70 seconds later.
